script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for MEME. Tuesday — here is your MEME recap. The portfolio closed six point one seven percent down on the session. Every primary holding finished in the red. This was a broad and punishing day.

SanDisk led the carnage — down twelve point six three percent in a single session, snapping a three-day winning streak and closing as one of the worst performers in the entire tech sector on Tuesday.

The Nasdaq sold off sharply on Tuesday, pressured by re-escalating geopolitical tensions after reports tied to a Trump administration Strait of Hormuz blockade rattled energy markets and sent oil prices spiking. That oil spike rippled directly into technology, space, and high-beta growth names — the exact profile of this portfolio. No specific index close figure was available in the macro feed, but sector-level damage was broad and deep.

SanDisk's twelve point six three percent decline is the headline number of Tuesday's session. The stock snapped a three-day winning streak and ranked among the ten most-avoided names in the market, according to reporting from Yahoo Finance. The catalyst appears macro rather than company-specific — the oil price spike tied to Strait of Hormuz tensions hammered memory sector sentiment broadly, pulling Micron and SK Hynix down alongside SanDisk. Despite the selloff, Wall Street moved in the opposite direction. Multiple analysts raised price targets on SanDisk, citing tight AI-driven supply conditions and a memory boom they argue has more runway than the market is currently pricing. One headline framed it directly: the memory boom may last longer than you think. For holders of this position at five percent of portfolio weight, the analyst divergence from price action is the critical tension to monitor heading into Wednesday.

Applied Optoelectronics was down six point seven zero percent on the session, the largest weight in this portfolio and one of the harder single-name hits on Tuesday. No company-specific news drove the move — this looks like broad tech selling tied to the macro environment. The stock's optical networking exposure keeps it sensitive to any shifts in AI infrastructure sentiment. Watch for any data center capital expenditure commentary in the coming sessions.

Redwire Corporation fell five point eight zero percent on Tuesday. Space names were broadly under pressure, and Redwire was no exception. A surprise milestone from China's space program rattled the narrative around American rocket and orbital dominance, contributing to sector-wide weakness. No Redwire-specific news drove the move independently.

AST SpaceMobile dropped seven point eight three percent, making it the second-worst performer in this portfolio on Tuesday behind SanDisk. Two headwinds converged — the China space milestone story and the oil spike hit space stocks simultaneously. Notably, despite the selloff, retail trader data showed SpaceX and AST SpaceMobile tied as the top two beaten-down space picks that traders were moving toward, each capturing thirty-three percent of retail buying interest in the sector according to Yahoo Finance. That is a contrarian signal worth tracking.

Nebius Group fell four point one six percent on Tuesday. A relevant development in the neocloud space emerged — Google announced a push to sell its tensor processing units deeper into fast-growing neocloud providers. As a neocloud-adjacent infrastructure name, Nebius sits in the middle of that competitive dynamic. This is a story that could cut both ways.

Bloom Energy was down four point five five percent on Tuesday, though the news backdrop for the stock was notably more constructive than the price action suggested. ClearBridge Investments highlighted alternative power demand as a driver in its second-quarter small and mid-cap commentary. A separate piece framed Bloom Energy as, quote, AI's emergency power button. The stock has gained twelve percent over the prior three months. Tuesday's decline looks more macro-driven than fundamentally driven.

Turning to TeraWulf — down four point nine two percent on the session. A comparative analysis piece weighed TeraWulf against Applied Digital in the neocloud and AI computing infrastructure space, framing both as companies helping ease the AI power bottleneck. No negative company-specific catalyst was identified. The move tracks the broader risk-off tone in high-beta technology names.

Lumentum Holdings fell four point two two percent on Tuesday. No company-specific news was available. As an optical components supplier with exposure to AI-driven network infrastructure buildout, Lumentum tends to move with broader technology sentiment. Tuesday's sector selling was the primary driver.

Over at IREN Limited — down five point two five percent. No specific news on IREN emerged during Tuesday's session. The name sits in the bitcoin mining and high-performance computing infrastructure space, a category that faced dual pressure from the macro selloff and energy price volatility. Watch energy cost dynamics closely given the oil move.

Three things to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any Federal Reserve speaker commentary — with oil spiking and geopolitical risk elevated, any signal on the rate path becomes more consequential for high-multiple growth names like those in this portfolio. Second, memory sector analyst notes following SanDisk's twelve point six three percent drop — multiple price target revisions were already flagged Tuesday night, and more are likely Wednesday morning. Third, developments on the Strait of Hormuz situation — oil price direction from here will set the tone for energy-sensitive and high-beta technology names across the entire portfolio.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for MEME. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the geopolitical headline flow overnight — oil and space sector sentiment will likely set the tone at Wednesday's open.
